examples.title: Remote Procedure Call (RPC) Basic Flow
examples.description: Demonstrates how RPC works to call a remote procedure as if it were local
examples.code:
FUNCTION RemoteProcedureCall(procedureName, parameters)
BEGIN
    // Pack parameters into message
    requestMsg := MARSHAL(procedureName, parameters)
    
    // Send to remote server
    serverAddress := LOOKUP(procedureName)
    responseMsg := SEND(network, serverAddress, requestMsg)
    
    // Wait for response
    WHILE NOT RECEIVED(responseMsg) DO
        WAIT(timeout)
    END WHILE
    
    // Unpack results
    result := UNMARSHAL(responseMsg)
    RETURN result
END
examples.explanation: RPC hides network communication complexity from programmers. Parameters are marshaled into a message, sent to server, processed, and results returned.
examples.timeComplexity: Depends on network latency and server processing

examples.title: Centralized Mutual Exclusion Algorithm
examples.description: Demonstrates centralized approach where coordinator grants access to critical section
examples.code:
// CLIENT SIDE
FUNCTION requestCS()
BEGIN
    SEND(coordinator, REQUEST, processID)
    WAIT_FOR_PERMIT()
    // Enter critical section
    criticalSection()
    SEND(coordinator, RELEASE, processID)
END

// COORDINATOR SIDE
VARIABLE queue := EMPTY
VARIABLE holder := NULL

ON RECEIVE_REQUEST(processID)
BEGIN
    IF holder == NULL THEN
        holder := processID
        SEND(processID, PERMIT)
    ELSE
        ENQUEUE(queue, processID)
    END IF
END

ON RECEIVE_RELEASE(processID)
BEGIN
    IF NOT EMPTY(queue) THEN
        holder := DEQUEUE(queue)
        SEND(holder, PERMIT)
    ELSE
        holder := NULL
    END IF
END
examples.explanation: Coordinator maintains queue of requests and grants permission to only one process at a time. Simple but coordinator is single point of failure.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is number of processes

examples.title: Simple Clock Synchronization (Cristian's Algorithm)
examples.description: Demonstrates client fetching time from time server and adjusting local clock
examples.code:
import time
import random

def get_synchronized_time(client_time, server_time, round_trip):
    # Calculate estimated server time when client's request arrives
    estimated_server_time = server_time + (round_trip / 2)
    # Adjust client clock
    time_offset = estimated_server_time - client_time
    return client_time + time_offset

# Simulated client-server interaction
def synchronize_with_server(client_socket, time_server):
    # Send request
    client_send_time = time.time()
    client_socket.send(b"GET_TIME")
    
    # Receive server time
    server_time = time_server  # Received from server
    
    # Calculate round trip time
    client_receive_time = time.time()
    round_trip = client_receive_time - client_send_time
    
    # Synchronize
    client_time = client_receive_time
    new_time = get_synchronized_time(client_time, server_time, round_trip)
    
    # Set system time (requires admin privileges)
    # os.system(f'settime -t {new_time}')
    return new_time

print("Synchronized time:", synchronize_with_server(None, 1234567890.0))
examples.explanation: Cristian's algorithm: client requests time from time server, calculates estimated one-way delay as half of round-trip time, adjusts local clock accordingly.

examples.title: Distributed Deadlock Detection - Wait-for Graph
examples.description: Shows how distributed deadlock detection works using wait-for graphs across machines
examples.code:
// Each site maintains local wait-for graph
STRUCTURE LocalWFG
BEGIN
    GRAPH graph
    TABLE localTransactions
END

// Build global wait-for graph
FUNCTION detectDeadlock()
BEGIN
    globalWFG := EMPTY
    
    // Collect local graphs from all sites
    FOR EACH site IN distributedSystem DO
        localGraph := site.getLocalWFG()
        globalWFG := MERGE(globalWFG, localGraph)
    END FOR
    
    // Check for cycles in global graph
    IF HAS_CYCLE(globalWFG) THEN
        cycle := EXTRACT_CYCLE(globalWFG)
        RETURN deadlockFound(cycle)
    ELSE
        RETURN noDeadlock()
    END IF
END

// On receiving deadlock notification
FUNCTION handleDeadlock(deadlockCycle)
BEGIN
    // Choose victim transaction
    victim := SELECT_VICTIM(deadlockCycle)
    
    // Abort victim
    ABORT_TRANSACTION(victim)
    
    // Release all resources held by victim
    FOR EACH resource IN victim.heldResources DO
        RELEASE(resource)
    END FOR
END
examples.explanation: Each site maintains local wait-for graph showing local transactions waiting for resources. Global deadlock detection merges these graphs and looks for cycles across sites.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n²) for cycle detection in merged graph
